contest.title: 2024-2025 ICPC, NERC, Northern Eurasia Finals (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Official NERC statement. There are 2n tree vertices, every service label occurs exactly twice, and each machine may participate in at most one swap. The total n over test cases is at most 1e5.
Official NERC tutorial establishes the maximum-tree-matching upper bound and constructs swaps by decomposing the service multigraph induced by matching edges into paths and cycles.
Independent solution cross-check. It gives a postorder greedy maximum matching and an explicit degree-two contraction implementation for path and cycle components.

Re-derived the matching upper bound, verified the virtual closing edge for path components, and exhaustively or deterministically checked 997 small tree/label instances; the optimum always equaled the tree maximum-matching size.

reference_candidates.notes: Codeforces status confirms this submission was accepted. Its source page was blocked by the Codeforces browser challenge, so it was not used as solution evidence.

annotation.summary: Given a tree whose 2n vertices contain two replicas of every service, output disjoint swaps maximizing the number of services whose two replicas finish on adjacent tree vertices.
annotation.constraints: Up to 1e5 test cases, 1 <= n <= 1e5 per test, and sum(n) <= 1e5. The tree has 2n vertices. Every label from 1 to n appears exactly twice, and every vertex may occur in at most one output swap.
annotation.core_idea: Every service that is good after the swaps occupies the two endpoints of one tree edge. Edges belonging to different good services are vertex-disjoint, so they form a matching and the answer is at most the maximum matching size of the tree. Find a maximum matching M in linear time. Build an auxiliary multigraph on service labels: every matched tree edge (u,v) contributes an edge between a_u and a_v. Since each service has only two replicas and every tree vertex belongs to at most one edge of M, every auxiliary vertex has degree at most two; hence every component is a path, cycle, loop, or two-edge multicycle. For a cycle, order the underlying machine positions alternately by matched edges and equal-service links, then pair positions symmetrically as in the official construction; these disjoint swaps make every matched edge monochromatic. For a path, each endpoint service has one occurrence outside M. Add a virtual edge between those two unused occurrences, apply the same cycle construction, and ignore the virtual edge afterward. Thus every edge of M becomes a good service edge, reaching the upper bound while using every machine at most once.
annotation.complexity: O(n) time and O(n) memory per test case; O(sum n) over all test cases.
tricks: Bound the objective by observing that successful service edges must form a tree matching.
Turn matching edges into a service multigraph of maximum degree two.
Solve auxiliary components independently as paths or cycles.
Close a path virtually with the unused replicas of its endpoint services.
Use a postorder greedy algorithm to reconstruct a maximum matching in a tree.

solution_variants.name: greedy-tree-matching-construction
solution_variants.summary: Root the tree and process vertices in postorder. Whenever a non-root vertex and its parent are both unmatched, match them. This produces a maximum tree matching. Decompose the induced degree-at-most-two service multigraph and construct disjoint swaps component by component.

solution_variants.name: tree-dp-matching-construction
solution_variants.summary: Use the standard two-state tree DP to compute and reconstruct a maximum matching, distinguishing whether a vertex is matched to one child. Apply the same path/cycle swap construction to the reconstructed matching.

tags.evidence: The exact optimum is the maximum matching size of the input tree, and the solution must compute and reconstruct such a matching before constructing swaps.
tags.description: Find, reconstruct, or exploit a maximum matching in a tree using its acyclic structure, usually through postorder greedy processing or tree DP. Do not use for generic matching in arbitrary graphs.
tags.created_reason: The existing graph/matching tag does not distinguish the reusable linear-time tree-matching pattern, while graph/tree alone does not indicate that matching is the optimization object.

tags.evidence: Computing the optimum value is insufficient: the main difficulty is constructing legal pairwise-disjoint swaps that attain every edge of the maximum matching.
The auxiliary service multigraph has maximum degree two, allowing the swap construction to process independent path and cycle components.
A postorder rule that matches an unmatched vertex with its unmatched parent reconstructs a maximum matching in a tree.
The input network being a tree is what makes maximum matching linear and supplies the matching upper bound used by the construction.
The maximum matching can alternatively be computed and reconstructed with the standard matched-to-child versus not-matched-to-child tree DP.
